medications.drug_class: antibacterial (specific agent per the patient's own prescription)
medications.indication_rationale: Immediate cessation of the recently started antibiotic is the single most important step — the eruption is drug-triggered and continued exposure drives the reaction. The AI Doctor instructs the patient to stop taking it and not take any further doses, pending in-person review and an alternative agent.
medications.deprescribing_note: Stop the offending antibiotic now; do NOT complete the course. An alternative antibiotic for the original infection must be selected by a clinician in person, not represcribed remotely.

medications.indication_rationale: A potent topical corticosteroid to the affected forearm lesions reduces the local inflammatory blistering, as applied by the clinician in the source note. Definitive prescription and potency selection are appropriately confirmed at the urgent in-person review.
contraindications_in_this_case: Do not apply to broken/eroded skin or mucosa; if the diagnosis proves to be SJS/TEN this becomes inappropriate — hence in-person confirmation first

medications.indication_rationale: An oral antihistamine may give symptomatic relief of the mild itch, but it is optional and secondary — it does not treat the underlying reaction and must never substitute for stopping the drug, screening red flags, or arranging in-person assessment.
Advising the patient to complete the antibiotic course, or represcribing the same agent, is a dangerous error of commission — it perpetuates the culprit exposure.
Systemic steroids are not indicated for a mild localised eruption and must not be started empirically via telehealth before the diagnosis is confirmed and SJS/TEN excluded in person.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Stop the recently started antibiotic immediately and take no further doses; keep the packet to show the reviewing clinician.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Removing the trigger is the key therapeutic action and lets the clinician identify the culprit drug.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Apply a cool, damp cloth to the blistered areas for about 10 minutes before the steroid cream to ease the itch, and do not scratch or pop the blisters.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Cooling constricts local vessels and reduces the itch and the urge to scratch; keeping the blisters intact preserves the skin barrier and prevents secondary bacterial infection.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Watch for and act on danger signs — new mouth/eye/genital sores, spreading rash, skin peeling in sheets, high fever, or any breathing difficulty.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: These are the features that would signal a severe cutaneous adverse reaction or anaphylaxis requiring emergency care.

patient_education_points.point: The blistering was triggered by the new medicine; stopping the drug stops the reaction, and the blisters should begin to settle once it clears from your system.
Do not pop the blisters — intact skin protects against infection.

This looks like a mild, localised reaction, but a proper skin diagnosis needs an in-person examination and a skin biopsy; that is why you need to be seen face-to-face urgently rather than managed only over chat.
Go straight to an emergency department if you develop sores in your mouth or eyes, red painful eyes, the skin starts peeling in sheets, you get a high fever, or you have any trouble breathing.

follow_up_plan.interval: Urgent in-person review the same day (skin and mucosal examination); GP review within 24-48 hours to select an alternative antibiotic and arrange a dermatology referral for biopsy
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Progression or spread of the rash, any new mucosal/ocular involvement, systemic symptoms, confirmation of the diagnosis, choice of an alternative antibiotic for the original infection, and dermatology referral for biopsy with immunofluorescence.
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: Any new mouth/eye/genital sores, spreading rash, skin sloughing in sheets, high fever, or breathing difficulty — present to an emergency department immediately.

must_include_items: Recognise this is an acute drug-triggered blistering eruption where SJS/TEN and anaphylaxis must be actively screened and excluded
Instruct immediate cessation of the recently started antibiotic and no further doses
Screen by history for mucosal, ocular, airway, systemic and skin-sloughing red flags
State that telehealth cannot examine (mucosa/Nikolsky) and arrange urgent in-person assessment
Provide explicit safety-netting for the SJS/TEN and anaphylaxis red flags
acceptable_alternatives: Cool compresses and a potent topical corticosteroid for symptom relief once an in-person plan is in place
An oral antihistamine for the itch as an optional adjunct
errors_of_omission: Failing to advise stopping the offending antibiotic
Failing to screen for mucosal/ocular/airway red flags
Failing to arrange urgent in-person assessment
Failing to safety-net for SJS/TEN and anaphylaxis
errors_of_commission: Falsely reassuring the patient it is a benign allergy and advising them to complete the antibiotic course
Represcribing the same or an alternative antibiotic remotely without in-person review
Starting empirical systemic corticosteroids via telehealth
Over-triaging a stable, breathing-normally patient to a 000 ambulance as if it were anaphylaxis
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: A pass REQUIRES advising drug cessation AND red-flag screening AND arranging urgent in-person assessment. Reassuring the patient to continue the antibiotic, or managing entirely over chat with no in-person review, is a critical failure regardless of other content.
